items.title: Economists on How Trump’s 2017 Tax Cuts Actually Unfolded | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Many provisions of President Trump’s signature 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, or TCJA, are set to expire at the end of 2025. Democrats warn that extending the cuts will come at the expense of programs that help Americans, with the Committee for a Responsible Budget estimating that Trump’s tax priorities would reduce government revenues between $5-11 trillion over 10 years. So how have the cuts affected individuals and businesses since 2017?

WSJ spoke to three economists who specialize in corporate taxes, pass-through business taxes and taxes for individuals about the fiscal impact of the cuts.

Chapters:
0:00 Trump’s 2017 tax cuts
0:36 Impact of corporate tax cuts
2:45 Impact of “pass-through” tax cuts
4:20 Impact of individual tax cuts
5:31 What’s next for Trump 2.0 tax bill?

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Trump #Tax #WSJ</article>

items.title: How Hamas Designed Gaza Hostage Releases to Shock Israel | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Israel claims to have killed around 10,000 Hamas militants and much of the group’s leadership including Yahya Sinwar after 15 months of war. But Hamas wants to show it is still in control of Gaza. As the first stage of the Israel-Hamas cease-fire comes to an end, WSJ analyzed how Hamas used images, props and symbolism during hostage releases to try to convey an image of enduring power.

Chapters:
0:00 Psychological warfare
0:32 Analyzing the releases
2:41 Why Hamas is doing this
4:24 Who Hamas is targeting

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Israel #Hamas #WSJ</article>

items.title: Resy’s CEO on How Amex Has Become Its Secret Weapon for Growth | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Since American Express acquired the online restaurant-reservation company Resy, dining has become an even bigger part of the Amex strategy. After acquiring two new platforms Tock and Rooam, Amex is doubling down on dining. WSJ sits down with Resy CEO and Head of American Express Global Dining Pablo Rivera to discuss this growth strategy, how it competes with competitors like OpenTable and Resy’s business model. Rivera also discusses how the company collects and uses data, where he sees the future of restaurant reservations headed and more. 

Chapters:
0:00 Resy and Amex
0:36 Opportunities when stepping in as CEO
1:09 Resy’s business model
2:02 Why Amex acquired Resy
2:28 Resy vs. competitors 
3:16 Reselling reservations, bots and security
5:01 Approach on cancellations and no-shows
6:23 How Resy collects and uses data
7:35 Amex’s recent acquisitions
8:03 What’s next for Resy?

#Resy #AmEx #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why Russia and China May Be Sabotaging Baltic Sea Infrastructure | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>In the Baltic Sea, a shadow war is raging between Russia and NATO. Commercial ships believed to be part of the Kremlin’s fleet stand accused of deliberately cutting subsea cables by dragging their anchors. There have also been incidents involving damage to other Baltic cables that have been traced to ships connected to China, sparking concerns about the level of collaboration between Moscow and Beijing.

WSJ explains how these acts of sabotage have turned the Baltic Sea into a flashpoint for the hybrid war and how NATO is ramping up patrols and military presence to protect critical underwater infrastructure.

Chapters:
0:00 Shadow war 
0:44 The strategy behind cable cuts
2:58 Russia’s role
4:15 What can NATO do?

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Russia #NATO #WSJ</article>

items.title: How Musk Is Running DOGE Like Twitter | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>In President Trump’s first month in office, a lot of work has been done by Elon Musk. Musk’s early marathon of government cuts at DOGE echoes his Twitter takeover back in 2022. In Musk’s first month leading the social media giant X, he cut roughly three quarters of the staff, brought in employees from his other companies and more. Will he be able to continue to make changes like this in the federal government?

WSJ breaks down how Musk’s business playbook doesn’t translate directly to the government and looks at what lies ahead for DOGE. 

Chapters:
0:00 Musk’s early moves
0:50 Move fast and flood the zone
2:30 Trimming the fat
4:40 Musk installing his own team
5:50 The results so far

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Trump #Musk #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why Trump and Republicans Hate the CFPB | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>In President Trump’s effort to shave down the federal government, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau was one of the first targets. But it’s always been controversial. Created in response to the 2008 financial crisis, the CFPB is essentially a watchdog for the American consumer. So what would happen to consumers if the agency designed to collect data from banks and financial companies to investigate fraud goes away?

WSJ explains what the agency does, why Republicans have always hated it and what happens now for consumers.

Chapters: 
0:00 Threats to the CFPB
0:31 What problem the CFPB was solving 
1:11 What the CFPB does
1:51 The criticisms 
4:16 The effect on consumers 

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Trump #CFPB #WSJ</article>

items.title: Starbucks Prices Are Rising: How Its New CEO Plans to Save the Company | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Starbucks is in a slump after a year of declining sales, boycotts and executive turnover. Now, the company has hired former Chipotle CEO Brian Niccol to turn the tide for the world’s largest coffee chain. So what led the chain of coffeehouses to this slump and how is the new CEO planning on turning it around?

WSJ’s restaurant reporter Heather Haddon sat down with Niccol to learn where he thinks Starbucks’s problems lie and how he plans to revamp mobile ordering on the app and store layout to bring customers back. 

Chapters:
0:00 Starbucks’ problem
1:17 Mobile ordering
3:07 Coffeehouse vibe
5:08 The condiment bar
6:05 How Starbucks got here
7:00 Niccol’s outlook

#Starbucks #Coffee #WSJ</article>

items.title: Inspectors General Fired by Trump Speak Out
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>President Trump recently fired a swath of Inspectors General in a late night purge of government watchdogs. The former Inspectors General for USAID, the Department of Veterans Affairs and the Department of Health and Human Services, who were all recently dismissed, sit down with WSJ’s Damian Paletta to discuss why it’s important for this role to remain independent. They also discuss the long-term implications of the mass firing, the lawsuit against the Trump administration, Elon Musk’s DOGE and more.

#Trump #DOGE #Shorts</article>

items.title: The Business Strategies Behind McDonald’s, Aldi, 7-Eleven and More | WSJ The Economics Of
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Why is Aldi one of the cheapest and fastest growing grocery stores in the U.S.? Why is 7-Eleven reinventing its U.S. stores to be more Japanese? Why do major meal kit companies like HelloFresh, Home Chef and Blue Apron lose about 90% of their customers in a year?

From McDonald’s and Shake Shack, to Liquid Death and Athletic Brewing, WSJ talked to CEOs and business leaders about their unique strategies to leading some of the most successful companies in the world.  

Chapters:
00:00 Aldi
06:18 Meal kit companies
14:50 Sweetgreen
22:17 Shake Shack
28:46 Cava
35:06 7-Eleven
42:25 Liquid Death
49:05 Athletic Brewing
56:19 McDonald’s

The Economics Of
How do the world's most successful companies generate revenue? In this explainer series, we'll dive into the surprising stories behind how businesses work--exploring everything from Costco's "treasure-hunt" model to the economics behind Amazon's AWS. 

#Economy #Business #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why So Many Crypto Experts Hate Trump's Meme Coins | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>President Trump’s cryptocurrency meme coin $TRUMP has already earned him billions of dollars on paper, but this is one of the most controversial investments ever made by a commander-in-chief. Trump has a long history of cashing in on his personal brand through products like shoes, guitars and bibles, but to many in the crypto world, his meme coin ventures are his most naked and predatory money grab yet. Once a bitcoin skeptic, Trump has promised to make the U.S. “the crypto capital of the planet.” 

WSJ explores why the Trump meme coin and Melania meme coin are so unprecedented and why they’re so controversial to the crypto industry. 

Chapters:
0:00 Trump’s meme coin
0:26 The Trump brand and crypto
2:44 Meme coin launch and ethical concerns
5:35 Meme coins, explained

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Trump #Crypto #WSJ</article>
